response_text:
:::writing
Every morning before sunrise, Captain Luis Ortega walks down the same weathered pier he has used for nearly thirty years. The boards creak in familiar places. The gulls gather on the pilings like impatient regulars. At the end of the dock sits the Maribel, the ferry he has piloted across the bay thousands of times, its white paint dulled by salt and seasons.

Ortega runs the short route between Port Calder and San Isidro, two small coastal towns that face each other across six miles of gray-green water. On a clear day the crossing takes just under forty minutes. In winter storms it can take longer, though Ortega shrugs off the difference. “The bay decides the schedule,” he says.

He started the job at twenty-nine, thinking it might be temporary work while he figured out what came next. The captain before him retired suddenly, the ferry company needed someone who knew boats, and Ortega had grown up fishing these waters with his father. One season turned into another, and before long he realized he had quietly become part of the daily machinery of both towns.

The ferry leaves Port Calder at 6:30 each morning. Ortega knows many of the passengers by name. There is Marta, a nurse who commutes to the clinic in San Isidro; Daniel, a carpenter who brings his tools in a blue crate; and on Fridays, a cluster of high school students who treat the upper deck like their personal lookout tower. Tourists come and go, but the regulars mark the calendar of the route.

Over the decades Ortega has learned the bay’s moods with the intimacy of a long conversation. He can read the water’s surface the way others read road signs: a line of darker ripples means a crosscurrent, a sudden silence in the wind often means fog is coming. In autumn he watches for drifting kelp that can wrap the propeller. In spring the water turns silver with anchovies, and pelicans follow the ferry as if escorting it across.

The job is repetitive in the way tides are repetitive—constant but never identical. Ortega keeps a small notebook in the wheelhouse where he records oddities: a whale sighting, a
